title: What Should You Test?
blurb: Given a method, the real skill is choosing which cases to cover. Sort each candidate into the kind of case it exercises. The legend explains each kind.

docs.label: Microsoft Learn — Characteristics of good unit tests

glossary.Happy path: The normal, valid input the method is built for — the case that should just work.
glossary.Edge & boundary: Tricky-but-valid inputs at the edges: zero, one, the max, empty-but-not-null, truncation, off-by-one territory.
glossary.Null & empty: Missing input: null references, empty strings, empty collections. Famously the source of the most bugs.
glossary.Throws (error): Invalid input the method should reject by throwing — tested with Assert.Throws, not by checking a return value.

levels.title: L1 · string Initials(string fullName)
levels.prompt: Initials("Ada Lovelace") should return "AL". Sort each candidate test by the kind of case it covers.

failStates.title: null is never the happy path
failStates.explain: A null name is missing input — it belongs in Null & empty. It's also exactly the case that throws NullReferenceException if the method forgets to guard it, so it's one of the most important tests to write.

failStates.title: A one-word name is an edge case
failStates.explain: The method is built for full names; a single word is valid but unusual — a boundary worth pinning down with its own test.

levels.title: L2 · int Divide(int a, int b)
levels.prompt: Divide(10, 2) should return 5. Sort each candidate test case.

items.label: Divide(int.MinValue, -1)
items.note: result can't fit in an int

failStates.title: Divide by zero throws
failStates.explain: 10 / 0 throws DivideByZeroException. That's an error case — test it with Assert.Throws<DivideByZeroException>, not by expecting a return value.

failStates.title: Truncation is a boundary
failStates.explain: 7 / 2 is 3, not 3.5 — integer division truncates toward zero. That surprising-but-correct behavior deserves its own edge-case test.

failStates.title: This one actually throws
failStates.explain: int.MinValue / -1 can't fit in an int and throws OverflowException — in C# this specific division always overflows. It's an error case, not just a large boundary value.
